title: Kiln wash preparation and shelf care
subtitle: Mixing and applying kiln wash to protect kiln shelves from glaze drips
excerpt: Kiln wash is a refractory coating brushed onto kiln shelves before every glaze firing to prevent glaze drips from bonding the pottery to the shelf. This tutorial covers mixing kiln wash to the correct consistency, applying it evenly, and maintaining shelf surfaces between firings.
sourceNotes: Kiln wash preparation and shelf care technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of refractory materials (typically equal parts alumina hydrate and calcined kaolin) applied to kiln shelves to prevent glaze drips from adhering. Also called shelf primer or bat wash.
A flat refractory slab inside the kiln that supports ceramic work during firing. Cordierite shelves are the standard for electric kilns.
A material that does not melt at kiln temperatures. Kiln wash, kiln shelves, and kiln furniture are all refractory materials.

content.text: before every glaze firing. If glaze runs down the side of a pot and contacts an unwashed shelf, the piece will bond to the shelf and cannot be removed without breaking. Kiln wash is
enough to remain powdery at glaze temperatures, releasing rather than fusing with the glaze.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when mixing or brushing kiln wash. Kiln wash materials (alumina hydrate, calcined kaolin) are fine ceramic powders. Never dry sweep kiln wash residues; wipe up with a damp cloth. Use a dedicated mixing bucket.

items.name: Kiln wash powder (pre-mixed or equal parts alumina hydrate and calcined kaolin)
Metal scraper or knife (for removing drips between firings)

content.text: Add water to the kiln wash powder gradually, stirring constantly, until the consistency is similar to single cream: thin enough to brush easily but thick enough to coat the shelf surface in a single coat. A common starting ratio is 500 g powder to 500 ml water, adjusted by eye. Wear the N95 mask throughout mixing and application.
Applying kiln wash to a new shelf
Brush a thin, even coat across the top surface of the shelf using the mop or hake brush in long overlapping strokes. Do not apply to the underside or edges.
Allow to dry completely, typically 30-60 minutes. Apply a second coat perpendicular to the first for even coverage.
For a new shelf, apply three coats, alternating direction. The shelf will mature with use; subsequent firings require only one top-up coat between firings.

After each glaze firing, inspect shelves for glaze drips. Scrape off any glaze deposits with a metal scraper or knife before applying fresh kiln wash. Do not simply paint over a glaze drip. The raised area will transfer to the next piece placed over it. Grind down stubborn spots with a wet abrasive stone, wipe clean with a damp cloth, and re-wash the affected area.
Use a dedicated kiln wash brush and keep it only for this purpose. Kiln wash residues on a glaze brush will contaminate the glaze.
For repeated high-temperature firings, add a small amount of silica to the mix. This helps the wash hold against flux-rich glazes.

items.symptom: Kiln wash powders off and sticks to the bottom of fired pots
items.cause: Kiln wash applied too thickly; powdery layer transfers to the pot base
items.fix: Apply thinner coats. The correct thickness is a translucent white film, not a thick white coating. Excess powdering means over-application

items.symptom: Shelf surface develops cracks in the kiln wash coat
items.cause: Wash applied too thick in one coat and dried unevenly
items.fix: Two or three thin coats dry without cracking. Thick coats crack as they dry, and the cracks deepen with each firing
